question_title: What will be the Strait of Hormuz 7-day average daily transit calls on June 25, 2026 per IMF PortWatch?
summary:
The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for 112 days since Feb 28, 2026. Despite the MOU signing on June 17 at Versailles and the US lifting its blockade on June 18, Iran re-closed the strait within hours and imposed new permit requirements through the PGSA (48-hour advance notice). As of June 19, PortWatch shows 0 transits (June 14 data), 8 of 9 major carriers continue rerouting, insurance markets are frozen, and naval mines remain uncleared.

My forecast for the June 25 7-day MA (averaging June 19-25) centers on a median of ~6 ships/day, reflecting a scenario mixture: ~15% chance the strait stays near-zero for the entire week (IRGC blocks implementation), ~33% chance a trickle of 2-5 ships/day gets through via permits, ~28% chance of slow ramp to 8-15 ships/day, and ~24% combined chance of faster reopening (15-60+ ships/day). The distribution is heavily right-skewed and wide, reflecting genuine uncertainty.

Calibrated against high-volume Polymarket markets ($28M): P(≥60)=1.5% on June 25 is consistent with Polymarket's 7.5% for ≥60 by June 30 (more days). P(≥30)=8.8% is consistent with earlier Metaculus estimates of ~15% for before June 28. The Seatrade headline reporting "2-month high" transits suggests some increase is starting, but Iran's obstructive post-deal behavior (re-closure, permit system, IRGC non-endorsement) indicates this will be slow.

factors.description: PortWatch at 0 transits as of June 14; strait effectively closed since Feb 28 (112 days). Status quo is near-zero traffic.
MOU signed June 17 at Versailles, US blockade lifted June 18. Pakistan says strait to reopen promptly. Creates diplomatic pressure for at least some reopening.
Iran re-closed strait after US lifted blockade and established PGSA with 48-hour advance notice requirement. IRGC has not endorsed the deal. Nightly drone attacks continued as of June 17.
Insurance markets frozen - no commercial coverage for Hormuz transit. Prevents most commercial operators from sending ships.
Naval mines in strait require clearing; France and UK deploying minesweepers. Mine-clearing takes 'a few weeks' per CNBC.
Seatrade headline 'Hormuz transits at 2-month high' (June 19) suggests SOME increase is already happening, potentially 5-15 ships.
8 of 9 major container carriers continue rerouting around Africa. Commercial shipping industry deeply cautious.
Polymarket P(7-day MA ≥60 by June 30) = 7.5% ($28M volume). Market very bearish on rapid reopening - dropped from 28.5% on deal day.
500+ ships waiting in Gulf + 2000 stranded create massive backlog pressure. IF strait opens partially, enormous pent-up demand.
Fitch assesses broad reopening unlikely before July 2026. CNBC says weeks to normalize, 10-15 days just to clear tanker backlog.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary: Strait of Hormuz 7-day Average Transit Calls on June 25, 2026**

| Percentile | Ships/day |
|---|---|
| P1 | 0.0 |
| P10 | 0.5 |
| P25 | 2.3 |
| P50 (median) | **6.2** |
| P75 | 10.3 |
| P90 | 20.7 |
| P99 | 62.5 |

**Key reasoning:** Despite the MOU signed June 17 at Versailles, the strait remains functionally closed as Iran re-imposed restrictions, insurance is unavailable, mines are uncleared, and the IRGC hasn't endorsed the deal. The most likely outcome is a modest trickle of ships (3-10/day) as the permit system begins operating, with significant right-tail risk if implementation proceeds faster than expected. The distribution is calibrated against Polymarket's deeply liquid ≥60-by-June-30 market (7.5%, $28M volume).

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the Strait of Hormuz 7-day moving average of daily transit calls for the date June 25, 2026, as reported by IMF PortWatch's chokepoint dataset for the Strait of Hormuz, accessible via the PortWatch Port Monitor (https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/port-monitor then Chokepoints then Strait of Hormuz). The value should be read from the chokepoint "Transit Calls" 7-day moving average series for June 25, 2026. For example, a 7-day moving average of 12.0 ships/day resolves as 12.
fine_print: If PortWatch does not report a 7-day moving average value but reports raw daily transit calls (n_total), the resolver will compute the simple average of the raw daily transit calls for June 19-25, 2026 inclusive; if some but not all of those days are reported, the average will be computed over the available days within that window. PortWatch updates weekly (typically Tuesdays); the value as displayed on or after the first PortWatch update that covers June 25, 2026 (expected by June 30, 2026) will be used, and later revisions will be ignored. If IMF PortWatch is permanently unavailable and publishes no data covering this date, the question is annulled.

factors.description: PortWatch at 0 transits as of June 14; strait effectively closed since Feb 28 (112 days). Status quo is near-zero traffic.
MOU signed June 17 at Versailles, US blockade lifted June 18. Pakistan says strait to reopen promptly. Creates strong diplomatic pressure for at least some reopening.
Iran re-closed strait after US lifted blockade and established PGSA with 48-hour advance notice requirement. IRGC has not endorsed the deal. Nightly drone attacks continued as of June 17.
Insurance markets frozen - no commercial coverage for Hormuz transit. This prevents most commercial operators from sending ships through.
Naval mines in strait require clearing operations; France and UK deploying minesweepers. Mine-clearing takes 'a few weeks' per CNBC.
Seatrade headline 'Hormuz transits at 2-month high' (June 19) suggests SOME increase is already happening, potentially 5-15 ships.
8 of 9 major container carriers continue rerouting around Africa. Commercial shipping industry deeply cautious.
Polymarket P(7-day MA ≥60 by June 30) = 7.5% ($28M volume). Market is very bearish on rapid reopening.
500+ ships waiting in Gulf + 2000 stranded ships create massive backlog pressure. IF the strait opens even partially, there's enormous pent-up demand to transit.
Fitch assesses broad reopening unlikely before July 2026. CNBC says weeks to normalize, 10-15 days just to clear tanker backlog.

input.anchor: Current PortWatch value is 0 ships/day (June 14). Pre-crisis was ~94. Status quo without any change would yield 0-2 on June 25.
input.assessment:
The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for 112 days. Despite the MOU signing on June 17, multiple critical barriers prevent rapid reopening: frozen insurance markets, uncleared mines, IRGC non-compliance, Iran re-imposing closure and establishing new permit requirements. The prediction market data is strongly bearish on any significant traffic recovery by end of June (Polymarket gives only 7.5% for reaching 60 ships/day by June 30).

However, the situation is not static. The MOU creates diplomatic momentum, Iran established a permit system (PGSA) suggesting intent to allow controlled traffic, and the Seatrade headline suggests some increase is starting. The enormous backlog of 500+ waiting ships means that even a small opening would quickly show some traffic.

My Monte Carlo simulation models 5 scenarios weighted by probability:
1. True near-zero (15%): IRGC blocks, 7-day MA ≈ 0-1
2. Trickle (33%): Few ships per day, 7-day MA ≈ 2-5
3. Slow reopening (28%): Permits work, ramp to 8-15, 7-day MA ≈ 6-10
4. Moderate reopening (15%): Good implementation, 7-day MA ≈ 12-20
5. Fast reopening (9%): Better than expected, 7-day MA ≈ 25-60+

The resulting distribution has median 6.2, P10 0.5, P90 20.7, and calibrates well against Polymarket data (P(≥30)=8.8% vs target 5-10%; P(≥60)=1.5% vs target 1-3%).
input.calibration_notes: Distribution calibrated against high-volume Polymarket markets ($28M for ≥60 by June 30). My P(≥30)=8.8% aligns with Metaculus's ~15% for 'before June 28' (broader window). My P(≥60)=1.5% implies cumulative ~5-7% by June 30 when including days after June 25, consistent with Polymarket's 7.5%. Straits.live's 8% for 'normal by end of June' also consistent.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether Iran's PGSA permit system actually processes requests and allows ships through. 2. Whether the Seatrade '2-month high' represents PortWatch-counted transits or just AIS sightings. 3. Whether insurance companies provide emergency coverage under government pressure. 4. Whether the IRGC cooperates with the MOU implementation or continues obstruction. 5. Whether mine-clearing proceeds fast enough for any ships to safely transit.
input.tool_audit:
Useful tools: research() returned excellent comprehensive data on the crisis, MOU, and current status. mcp__search__web_search found critical Polymarket data with prices and history, Wikipedia articles with timeline updates, and Seatrade headline. mcp__search__fetch_url worked well for straits.live, global-energy-flow.com, and CNBC article content extraction. execute_code ran Monte Carlo simulation effectively.

Empty/partial results: Seatrade-maritime.com and timesofisrael.com returned 403 errors. Polymarket bracket market didn't show all brackets. Could not directly access PortWatch data due to its ArcGIS-based dashboard.

Failures: None critical. The 403s were worked around via web search snippets.
input.update_triggers: 1. Reports of actual commercial vessels completing Hormuz transits post-MOU (would shift up significantly). 2. Insurance companies announcing emergency coverage for Hormuz (major upward shift). 3. IRGC publicly endorsing the MOU (very unlikely but would be transformative). 4. New military escalation or collapse of MOU (shift toward 0). 5. Real-time PortWatch data showing any non-zero transits post-June 14.
input.process_reflection:
The research sub-agent was exceptionally useful here, returning thorough synthesis of a complex geopolitical situation with prediction market data included. The multi-source web search for Polymarket markets with structured API data was very valuable for calibration.

Main friction: couldn't access the actual PortWatch dashboard data (ArcGIS-based, not text-extractable). Also couldn't access two paywalled/protected articles (Seatrade, Times of Israel) that had potentially important details. The Polymarket multi-bracket markets only returned one bracket's probability - would be helpful to have a tool that extracts all bracket probabilities for multi-outcome markets.

The scenario-based Monte Carlo approach worked well for this type of question where there are discrete possible futures (closed vs. various reopening speeds) rather than continuous variation.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Checked all 10 factors against the research trace. No fabricated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment.**

### Factor-by-factor verification:

1. **PortWatch at 0 transits (Factor 1)**: Confirmed by research entry and straits.live fetch — "PortWatch official count: 0 transits on June 14, 2026 (most recent published data)." ✅

2. **MOU signed June 17 at Versailles (Factor 2)**: Confirmed by Wikipedia extract in the trace — "On 17 June, Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian signed the memorandum of understanding to end the war, with Trump signing it during dinner with French President Emmanuel Macron at the Palace of Versailles following the G7 summit." US blockade lifted June 18 confirmed by CENTCOM announcement in same source. ✅

3. **Iran re-closed strait, PGSA 48-hour requirement (Factor 3)**: Confirmed by straits.live fetch — "Iran re-closed the Strait: Within hours of the US lifting its blockade, Iran reimposed closure restrictions" and newkerala.com search result confirming PGSA. ✅

4. **Insurance markets frozen (Factor 4)**: Confirmed by multiple research sources — "No commercial insurance coverage available for Hormuz transit" and "No commercial insurers were willing to cover Hormuz transit even under military escort as of June 17." ✅

5. **Naval mines, minesweeper deployment (Factor 5)**: Confirmed by CNBC article fetch — "mine clearance: 'A few weeks' if mine clearance is needed" and research entry noting France/UK minesweeper deployments. ✅

6. **Seatrade "2-month high" (Factor 6)**: The headline appeared in search results but the article returned HTTP 403. The agent only saw the headline "Hormuz transits at 2-month high, Iran issues passage instructions" — the "potentially 5-15 ships" is the agent's inference, not sourced data. However, confidence is appropriately set at 0.6 (lowest among positive factors), and the agent explicitly notes in reasoning that the article content was inaccessible. Minor concern but properly caveated. ✅

7. **8 of 9 carriers rerouting (Factor 7)**: Confirmed by straits.live — "8 of 9 major container carriers continue rerouting." ✅

8. **Polymarket 7.5% (Factor 8)**: Confirmed by Polymarket API returning probability: 0.075 and volume: $27.9M on June 19. The earlier research from June 17 showed 18.5%, so the agent correctly captured the sharp decline to 7.5% using the more recent data. ✅

9. **500+ ships waiting (Factor 9)**: Confirmed by Argus Media data in research and straits.live data showing 470 anchored/stopped vessels. ✅

10. **Fitch July 2026 assessment (Factor 10)**: Confirmed in multiple research entries. CNBC article confirms "10-15 days estimated to clear the tanker backlog." ✅

### Double-counting check:
No problematic double-counting. Factors 3 (IRGC/PGSA), 4 (insurance), 5 (mines), and 7 (carrier rerouting) are distinct barriers even though they all contribute to low traffic. Each represents a separate obstacle that could independently prevent reopening.

### Resolution criteria alignment:
The agent correctly identifies that the question resolves to IMF PortWatch's 7-day MA for June 25, properly accounts for PortWatch's AIS-based methodology (noting 5-10 ships transit with AIS off and are not counted), and addresses the publication lag.

### Probability assessment:
The forecast (median 6.2, P10=0.5, P90=20.7) is well-calibrated against market data:
- Agent's P(≥60 on June 25) = 1.5% is consistent with Polymarket's 7.5% for ≥60 by June 30 (which includes more days)
- Agent's P(≥30 on June 25) = 8.8% is consistent with the previous binary forecast of 15% for ≥30 before June 28
- The Monte Carlo simulation was run with explicit calibration against market data

### Cross-question consistency:
Previous forecast (44095, June 17) gave 15% for "Will 7-day MA reach 30 before June 28?" Current forecast's P(≥30 on June 25) = 8.8% is consistent — lower probability for a single day vs. any of 3 days, with expected ramp-up pattern.

### Counterargument quality:
Strong. The agent identifies substantive risks: PortWatch measurement limitations (AIS-off vessels not counted), the pattern of Iran re-imposing restrictions after apparent openings, the 48-hour notice drag on the 7-day average, and Polymarket's increasing pessimism post-deal. This is a genuine attempt to break the forecast, not a token gesture.

### Confidence assessment:
0.35 is appropriate for this unprecedented geopolitical situation with massive scenario uncertainty.

### Tool audit:
Agent's claim of "Seatrade-maritime.com and timesofisrael.com returned 403 errors" matches the 2 fetch_url errors in the metrics (6 calls, 33.3% = 2 errors). "None critical" for failures is accurate.
